parentQuestion: A patient has ptosis (drooping eyelid), a dilated pupil, and an eye that looks down and out. Which nerve is damaged?

scaffolds.explain: Ptosis = DROOPING EYELID. The levator palpebrae muscle that lifts the upper eyelid is controlled by CN III. When CN III is damaged, the eyelid falls down.

wrongExplains.0: A dilated pupil is called mydriasis, not ptosis. Ptosis specifically refers to eyelid drooping.
wrongExplains.2: Double vision is called diplopia. Ptosis is about the eyelid position, not vision quality.
wrongExplains.3: Eye pain has various causes but isn't called ptosis. Ptosis = droopy lid.

scaffolds.q: Which nerve controls the muscle that LIFTS the upper eyelid?
scaffolds.explain: CN III (oculomotor) controls the levator palpebrae superioris — the muscle that LIFTS the upper eyelid. Damage causes the lid to droop (ptosis).

wrongExplains.0: CN II carries VISION. It doesn't control any muscles, including eyelid muscles.
wrongExplains.2: CN VII closes the eye (orbicularis oculi). CN III opens/lifts the eyelid. Different actions, different nerves.
wrongExplains.3: CN VI controls lateral eye movement (abduction). It doesn't affect the eyelid.

scaffolds.q: A dilated pupil (mydriasis) that won't constrict suggests damage to which nerve?
scaffolds.explain: CN III controls pupil CONSTRICTION through the sphincter pupillae muscle. When CN III is damaged, the pupil can't constrict — it stays dilated ('blown pupil').

wrongExplains.0: CN II carries visual information TO the brain. It doesn't control the pupil muscles directly. CN III does.
wrongExplains.2: CN V handles facial sensation and chewing. It has nothing to do with the pupil.
wrongExplains.3: CN VIII handles hearing and balance. It doesn't affect the pupil at all.

scaffolds.q: When CN III is damaged, the eye drifts 'down and out.' Why?
options: CN III pulls the eye down and out
The remaining working muscles (CN IV and VI) pull unopposed
scaffolds.explain: CN III controls most muscles that move the eye UP and INWARD. When paralyzed, CN IV (superior oblique) pulls DOWN and CN VI (lateral rectus) pulls OUT — unopposed.

wrongExplains.0: CN III actually pulls the eye UP and IN. When it fails, the eye goes the opposite direction.
wrongExplains.2: Gravity plays a minimal role. The deviation is caused by the muscle imbalance from CN IV and VI pulling without CN III resistance.
wrongExplains.3: The patient has no control over this. It's an involuntary result of muscle paralysis.

scaffolds.q: CN III controls how many of the six eye muscles?
scaffolds.explain: CN III controls FOUR eye muscles: Superior rectus, inferior rectus, medial rectus, and inferior oblique. That's why CN III damage causes such dramatic eye deviation.

wrongExplains.0: One muscle is controlled by CN IV (superior oblique). CN III controls four.
wrongExplains.1: Two is not enough. CN III is the major eye movement nerve, controlling four of six muscles.
wrongExplains.3: Six muscles are split between three nerves: CN III (four), CN IV (one), CN VI (one).

scaffolds.q: The classic triad of CN III palsy is: Ptosis, mydriasis, and ___.
scaffolds.explain: The CN III palsy triad: (1) Ptosis (can't lift lid), (2) Mydriasis (dilated pupil), (3) Eye DOWN and OUT. All three symptoms point to one damaged nerve.

wrongExplains.0: Deafness involves CN VIII. CN III damage doesn't affect hearing.
wrongExplains.2: Facial numbness involves CN V. CN III damage doesn't affect facial sensation.
wrongExplains.3: Loss of smell involves CN I. CN III damage doesn't affect olfaction.

scaffolds.q: Why do these THREE symptoms (ptosis, dilated pupil, eye deviation) all occur together?
options: One nerve (CN III) controls all three functions
scaffolds.explain: CN III is a MULTI-FUNCTION nerve: It lifts the eyelid, constricts the pupil, AND moves most eye muscles. Damage to this one nerve causes all three symptoms.

wrongExplains.0: One nerve damage explains all three. CN III handles lid, pupil, and most eye movement.
wrongExplains.2: This pattern is not coincidental — it's a classic, predictable result of CN III damage.
wrongExplains.3: Peripheral nerve damage (CN III itself) can cause this triad without brain involvement.

scaffolds.q: A posterior communicating artery aneurysm can cause CN III palsy. Why?
options: The artery is far from CN III
The aneurysm compresses CN III as it passes nearby
CN III is inside the artery
scaffolds.explain: The posterior communicating artery runs VERY CLOSE to CN III. An aneurysm (bulging blood vessel) can compress the nerve, causing the classic palsy. This is a medical emergency.

wrongExplains.0: The artery is very close to CN III — that's exactly why aneurysms there cause CN III problems.
wrongExplains.2: Aneurysms absolutely can compress nearby nerves. CN III palsy from aneurysm is well-documented.
wrongExplains.3: CN III is a nerve that runs outside blood vessels, but close enough to be compressed by bulging ones.

scaffolds.q: If a patient suddenly develops this triad, why is it potentially an emergency?
options: A brain aneurysm could be compressing the nerve and might rupture
It always goes away on its own
scaffolds.explain: Sudden CN III palsy with pupil involvement can indicate a BRAIN ANEURYSM about to rupture. This is life-threatening and requires immediate imaging and treatment.

wrongExplains.0: Sudden CN III palsy CAN be serious — it might signal an aneurysm that could rupture and cause stroke or death.
wrongExplains.2: While some CN III palsies (like diabetic) may improve, sudden onset with pupil involvement needs urgent evaluation.
wrongExplains.3: This is far more than cosmetic — the underlying cause (aneurysm) can be fatal if not treated.
